item.title: 【Your Merge Forward guide to KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Amsterdam】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 阿姆斯特丹的 Merge Forward 指南
item.description:
【

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 is coming to Amsterdam, and Merge Forward is one of several groups bringing community, conversation, and a full lineup of sessions and events.

Whether you’re a first-time attendee or a KubeCon + CloudNativeCon regular, Merge Forward could be your destination for community, empowerment, and fun. Stop by to chat with volunteers about community, careers, and contribution, and learn how we’re empowering contributors in the cloud native ecosystem. Find your people and build a resilient support network at one of our Community Hub sessions for underrepresented groups and allies. 

What’s Merge Forward 

Merge Forward is a CNCF Technical Community Group focused on empowering cloud native contributors from underrepresented groups. Not yet a year old, we provide a platform for networking and collaboration around careers, contribution, and especially diversity and inclusion. At KubeCon + CloudNativeCon, we’ll host community gatherings, including Women in Cloud Native, Neurodiversity, and the LGBTQ+ community, and more.

Where to Find Us: Kiosk P-17B on Monday

Our home base at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon is Kiosk P-17B in the Project Pavilion (Halls 1–5).

When: Monday, all day

Stop by to:

  • Chat with Merge Forward volunteers and speakers about community, careers, and contribution
  • Learn how we’re empowering contributors in the cloud-native ecosystem
  • Meet fellow community builders and get the inside scoop on what’s happening

We’ll be there the full day. Come say hi.

What’s on: The Community Hub and Merge Forward gathering

Merge Forward will be hosting various interactive Community Hub sessions focused on diversity and inclusion. Don’t miss a thing—add all sessions to your schedule. Merge Forward is also co-hosting its first gathering during KubeCon + CloudNativeCon. Join us to meet the team, make new connections. Add everything to your schedule today.

Community Hub session 

Tuesday

  • 11 am: Merge + Meet: Kickoff Meeting for Underrepresented Groups and Allies 
  • 12:30 pm: Deep Roots: Black, Indigenous, and People of Color Community Gathering
  • 4 pm: Women’s Community Gathering

Wednesday

  • 11:45 am: Designing Slides You Can’t See (But Can Still Understand)
  • 1:15 pm: 🤟 Sign Language Crash Course
  • 2:30 pm: LGBTQ+ Community Gathering
  • 3:45 pm: Neurodiversity at Work: Practical Tools, Open Source Paths, and Inclusive Community Building
  • 5 pm: When Speaking Is Not Linear: What “Effective Communication” Really Means

Thursday

  • 12:15 pm: Your Voice on Stage: The Iterative Pitch Lab 
  • 1:15 pm: Non-Code Contribution Crash Course

Your Cheat Sheet

See You in Amsterdam

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 is shaping up to be a big week for the cloud-native community. Merge Forward will be there — at the kiosk, in the Community Hub, and at the gathering.

Add these to your calendar. Come find us. And if you see us around, say hi.

Connect with Merge Forward

The conversation doesn’t stop in Amsterdam. Keep up with Merge Forward’s mission to empower cloud native contributors by joining our community online.

  • Slack: CNCF #merge-forward
  • LinkedIn: Merge Forward

We look forward to connecting with you at the kiosk, throughout the week at the Community Hub sessions, and especially at the party. Add everything to your calendar today, and we’ll see you in Amsterdam!

】

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 即将在阿姆斯特丹举行，Merge Forward 是带来社区、对话以及全套会议和活动的几个团体之一。

无论您是首次参加者还是 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 的常客，Merge Forward 都可以成为您获得社区、赋权和乐趣的目的地。顺便与志愿者讨论社区、职业和贡献，并了解我们如何为云原生生态系统中的贡献者提供支持。在我们针对代表性不足的群体和盟友的社区中心会议之一中找到您的员工并建立一个有弹性的支持网络。 

什么是向前合并

Merge Forward 是一个 CNCF 技术社区小组，致力于为代表性不足的群体中的云原生贡献者提供支持。成立还不到一年，我们提供了一个围绕职业、贡献，尤其是多样性和包容性进行交流和协作的平台。在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 上，我们将举办社区聚会，包括云原生女性、神经多样性和 LGBTQ+ 社区等。

在哪里找到我们：周一 Kiosk P-17B

我们在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 上的大本营是位于项目展馆（1-5 号展厅）的 Kiosk P-17B。

时间：周一全天

停下来：

  • 与 Merge Forward 志愿者和演讲者讨论社区、职业和贡献
  • 了解我们如何为云原生生态系统中的贡献者提供支持
  • 与其他社区建设者会面，了解最新动态

我们会在那里呆一整天。过来打个招呼。

活动内容：社区中心和 Merge Forward 聚会

Merge Forward 将举办各种互动社区中心会议，重点关注多样性和包容性。不要错过任何事情——将所有课程添加到您的日程安排中。 Merge Forward 还在 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 期间共同举办了第一次聚会。加入我们，认识团队，建立新的联系。将所有内容添加到您今天的日程安排中。

社区中心会议

星期二

  • 上午 11 点：合并 + 会议：代表性不足的群体和盟友启动会议 
  • 下午 12:30：深入根源：黑人、原住民和有色人种社区聚会
  • 下午 4 点：妇女社区聚会

星期三

  • 上午 11:45：设计您看不到（但仍能理解）的幻灯片
  • 下午 1:15：🤟手语速成课程
  • 下午 2:30：LGBTQ+ 社区聚会
  • 下午 3:45：工作中的神经多样性：实用工具、开源路径和包容性社区建设
  • 下午 5 点：当说话不线性时：“有效沟通”的真正含义是什么

星期四

  • 下午 12:15：舞台上的声音：迭代推介实验室 
  • 下午 1:15：非代码贡献速成课程

您的备忘单

阿姆斯特丹见

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 对于云原生社区来说将是重要的一周。 Merge Forward 将会在那里 — 在信息亭、社区中心和聚会上。

将这些添加到您的日历中。来找我们吧。如果您在附近看到我们，请打个招呼。

通过合并转发进行连接

谈话并没有在阿姆斯特丹停止。通过加入我们的在线社区来履行 Merge Forward 的使命，为云原生贡献者提供支持。

  • Slack：CNCF #merge-forward
  • LinkedIn：合并转发

我们期待在信息亭、整个星期的社区中心会议、尤其是聚会上与您联系。今天将所有内容添加到您的日历中，我们将在阿姆斯特丹见到您！

item.title: 【Crossplane and AI: The case for API-first infrastructure】Crossplane 和 AI：API 优先基础设施的案例
item.description:
【

AI-assisted development has changed the way engineers create and commit code. But writing code is no longer the bottleneck. The bottleneck is everything that happens after git push.

From infrastructure provisioning, policy enforcement, day-two operations, drift, compliance, to cross-team coordination. That still requires multiple steps, and no new tool will fix it. This is an architecture problem. AI needs APIs, not UIs, and most platforms still aren’t built that way.

Current platforms

Talk to almost any organization, and you’ll hear that the desired state lives in Git, while the actual state lives in cloud providers. Policies are buried in pipeline configs. Organizational knowledge exists in wikis no one reads and in engineers who eventually leave.

This has worked up to now because humans worked with humans to navigate the context switching and informal coordination required to get the job done. People fill in the gaps, ask the questions, and translate intent across systems.

But in a world where AI agents are embedded into our organizations, this workflow breaks down. The agent hits a wall, not because it lacks capability, but because the platform wasn’t built for programmatic access. It was built for humans who can compensate for inconsistency.

Agents require a unified, structured, machine-readable interface. They need explicit governance rules, readable historical patterns, and discoverable dependencies. Without that structure, autonomy stalls.

Platforms built on declarative control

Kubernetes introduced a simple but powerful control pattern that changes this entirely. Every resource follows a consistent schema:

yaml

apiVersion: example.crossplane.io/v1
kind: Database
metadata:
  name: user-db
spec:
  engine: postgres
  storage: 100Gi

Desired state lives in spec, actual state is reflected in status, and controllers observe the difference and reconcile continuously. That reconciliation is consistent and automatic; no human is required to coordinate convergence.

Crossplane extends this model beyond containers to all infrastructure and applications: cloud databases, object storage, networking, SaaS systems, clusters, and custom platform APIs. The result isn’t just infrastructure-as-code. It’s your entire platform, infrastructure, and applications as a single API. That difference matters.

The three core elements that make this work in practice:

  • Desired state: the declarative specification of what we think the world should be. (Example: The frontend service should have 3 replicas with 2 GB of memory each.)
  • Actual state: the operational reality of what exists in the infrastructure. (Example: The frontend service has 2 healthy replicas, 1 pending.)
  • Policy: the rules and governance that constrain operations. (Example: Production changes require approval between 9 AM and 5 PM PST.)

Controllers continuously reconcile desired state with actual state, and policy is enforced at execution rather than left to manual review. Context becomes part of the system, not something external to it.

Why this model works for agents

An AI agent interacting with a Crossplane-managed platform doesn’t need to orchestrate workflows across multiple systems. It interacts with a single API surface.

It can discover resource types via the Kubernetes API, inspect status fields for real-time operational state, watch resources for change events, and submit declarative intent. Since reconciliation handles mechanical execution, agents don’t need to coordinate step-by-step logic; they just declare intent and let controllers handle convergence.

This separation of concerns is critical. Controllers handle mechanics, while agents focus on higher-level reasoning. Without a control plane, agents become fragile orchestrators. With one, they become declarative participants.

When the entire platform is accessible through a single, consistent API, the agent has everything it needs. No Slack messages and no tribal knowledge required.

Policy at the point of execution

In fragmented platforms, governance follows lots of procedures: reviews, tickets, Slack threads. In a Kubernetes-native control plane, governance is architectural.

RBAC controls who can act. Admission controllers validate changes before they’re persisted. Policy engines such as OPA and Kyverno enforce constraints at runtime. Crossplane compositions encode organizational patterns directly into APIs. Every change flows through the same enforcement path, no hidden approval steps, no undocumented exception paths.

This removes ambiguity for agents entirely. The system defines what is allowed. Agents operate within clearly defined boundaries, and the platform enforces them automatically.

Crossplane 2.0: Full-stack control

With Crossplane 2.0, compositions can include any Kubernetes resource, not just managed infrastructure. That means a single composite API can provision infrastructure, deploy applications, configure networking, set up observability, and define operational workflows,  all in one place.

apiVersion: platform.acme.io/v1
kind: Microservice
metadata:
  namespace: team-api
  name: user-service
spec:
  image: acme/user-service:v1.2.3
  database:
    engine: postgres
    size: medium
  ingress:
    subdomain: users

Behind that abstraction may live RDS instances, security groups, deployments, services, ingress rules, and monitoring resources. To a human developer or an AI agent, it’s a single API. That consistency is what enables automation to scale safely.

Day-two operations follow the same pattern. Crossplane’s Operation types bring declarative control to scheduled upgrades, backups, maintenance, and event-driven automation:

apiVersion: ops.crossplane.io/v1alpha1
kind: CronOperation
metadata:
  name: weekly-db-maintenance
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * 0"
  operationTemplate:
    spec:
      pipeline:
        - step: upgrade
          functionRef:
            name: function-database-upgrade

Operational workflows are now first-class API objects. Agents can inspect them, trigger them, observe their status, and propose modifications. No need for hidden runbooks.

Where to start

This doesn’t require a start-from-scratch migration. Bring core infrastructure under declarative control first. Your existing resources don’t need to be replaced; they just need to be unified behind a consistent API.

For teams using AI-assisted development, engineers express intent and iterate quickly as tools accelerate implementation. As deployment decouples from release, with changes shipping behind feature flags and systems reconciling toward the desired state, the platform must be deterministic and self-correcting, not reliant on someone catching drift or running the right command at the right time.

That is what a declarative control plane provides. Crossplane ensures that intent has somewhere safe, structured, and deterministic to land. Without it, AI will always be bolted onto human-centric workflows. With it, agents become first-class participants in infrastructure operations.

And that starts with a consistent API.

And that starts with a consistent API. Get started by checking out the Crossplane Docs, attending a community meeting, or watching CNCF’s Cloud Native Live on Crossplane 2.0 – AI-Driven Control Loops for Platform Engineering.

】

人工智能辅助开发改变了工程师创建和提交代码的方式。但编写代码不再是瓶颈。瓶颈是 git Push 之后发生的一切。

从基础设施配置、策略执行、第二天运营、漂移、合规性到跨团队协调。这仍然需要多个步骤，而且没有新工具可以解决这个问题。这是一个架构问题。人工智能需要 API，而不是 UI，而大多数平台仍然不是这样构建的。

当前平台

与几乎任何组织交谈，您都会听说所需的状态位于 Git 中，而实际状态位于云提供商中。策略隐藏在管道配置中。组织知识存在于无人阅读的维基百科和最终离开的工程师之中。

到目前为止，这种方法一直有效，因为人类与人类合作来完成工作所需的上下文切换和非正式协调。人们填补空白，提出问题，并跨系统翻译意图。

但在人工智能代理嵌入到我们的组织中的世界中，这个工作流程就崩溃了。代理碰壁，不是因为它缺乏能力，而是因为该平台不是为编程访问而构建的。它是为能够弥补不一致的人类而构建的。

代理需要一个统一的、结构化的、机器可读的界面。他们需要明确的治理规则、可读的历史模式和可发现的依赖关系。如果没有这种结构，自治就会停滞不前。

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 2026: End User TAB recommendations and tips】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 2026：最终用户 TAB 建议和提示
item.description:
【

KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 2026 in Amsterdam will bring the cloud native community together with projects, vendors and end users in what is one of the largest open source events.

In this post, we highlight sessions and activities that may be of particular interest to end users, including dedicated discussion forums and recommendations from members of the CNCF End User Technical Advisory Board (TAB) along with practical tips for making the most of the event.

End User content

The TAB has thought of different ways where the experience of end users at KubeCon + CloudNativecan be enhanced This will be a gradual process, but some of the ideas have been implemented in advance of  Kubecon + CloudNativeCon Europe in Amsterdam.

Make sure you do not miss these opportunities to discuss with other end users.

Birds of Feather (BoF)

We will have three BoF sessions targeting end users, as a space to focus and discuss how we’re targeting specific topics and come out with best practices and gaps to be tackled. For this Kubecon + CloudNativeCon we’ll be covering:

  • BoF: AI Infrastructure and Platform: LLMs, Agentic AI
    • Convener: Joseph Sandoval
    • Best practices for generic MLOps on cloud native platforms, common pain points of running such workloads on cloud native infra, gaps in the existing ecosystem
  • BoF: Infrastructure Optimization for GPUs / Inference / Training / Networking
    • Convener: Ricardo Rocha
    • Low level runtime optimizations, CPU/GPU NUMA affinity and pinning, low latency networking, support for heterogeneous hardware
  • BoF: AI Observability
    • Convener: Alolita Sharma
    • Support for observability of inference, training and agent workloads, tools being used today in production, gaps in the existing ecosystem

Town Hall

We’ll have our usual town hall where end users can come forward with questions to the TAB. But this time we change the format, starting the session with a series of short 5min presentations that should trigger discussion between the TAB and the audience.

Poster sessions

The effort continues on publishing more reference architectures from our end users, you can check them here. We’re giving the possibility for owners of published architectures to have a poster during the poster session to showcase their own publications.

Pass by and you can directly ask your questions.

End User reception

On Tuesday 17:30 – 18:30 there will be a reception for CNCF end user members. Join us at the Grand Cafe, Ground Level – Entrance K side of the building.

Session highlights

Below you can find session recommendations and additional tips on how to make the best of your KubeCon + CloudNativeConexperience this year, from the members of the TAB.

Don’t hesitate to reach out and grab us for a chat in the hallway!

Ahmed Bebars

1000 Services, 1 Year, 0 Downtime: Airbnb’s Zonal Cluster Migration

Speakers: Sunny Beatteay, Airbnb

Why I want to see it: I’m always looking for real stories of large organizations moving critical workloads without reader‑visible downtime. Airbnb talks through how they migrated around a thousand services to a zonal topology in a year with zero downtime. I’m particularly interested in the migration strategies, blast‑radius thinking, and what they’d do differently next time.

Kubernetes Autopsy: Live Debugging a Cluster Meltdown (Disney+ Hotstar)

Speakers: Sandeep Kanabar (Gen), Aditi Gupta (Disney+ Hotstar), Anshika Gupta (Independent)

Why I want to see it: News and media platforms experience traffic spikes and hard SLOs similar to streaming. I’m keen to hear how Disney+ Hotstar approached a real Kubernetes cluster meltdown: what their telemetry showed (or failed to show), how they debugged under pressure, and what permanent changes they made to avoid a repeat.

Smart Routing at Scale: How Spotify’s XDS Control Plane Cut 75% of Cross‑Zone Traffic

Speakers: Yannick Epstein, Anya Hristova – Spotify

Why I want to see it: Multi‑AZ and multi‑region setups easily leak money through unnecessary cross‑zone traffic. Spotify’s talk on how they used an XDS control plane to cut cross‑zone traffic by 75% is exactly the kind of deep technical routing and control‑plane story that can translate into both cost savings and better latency for users.

We Deleted Our Observability Stack and Rebuilt It With OTEL: 12 Engineers to 4 at 20K+ Clusters (DigitalOcean)

Speakers: Yash Sharma, Kunju Perath – DigitalOcean

Why I want to see it: Observability is often where cost, complexity, and toil quietly accumulate. DigitalOcean’s story of rebuilding their observability stack around OpenTelemetry for 20K+ clusters—while shrinking the team from 12 to 4—sounds like a masterclass in focusing on the right signals, rethinking architecture, and balancing visibility with cost.

Alolita Sharma

Collisions in the Dark: Illuminating the 95% of Kubeflow You Can’t See

Speakers: Amine Lahouel and Laura Llinares, CERN

Why I want to see it: This is an interesting session where CERN scientists will be discussing what they need from an observability perspective on the performance of their ML platform infrastructure. Gaining deep, actionable insights into the performance, resource consumption, and overall health of the entire system are major KPIs for end-users. Understanding CERN’s takeaways and challenges in measuring ML platform performance with these KPIs will be valuable.

Discover Cortex: High Scalability Metrics in 2026

Speakers: Friedrich Gonzalez and Charlie Le, Apple

Why I want to see it: This session aims to dive into the latest features in Cortex, a multi-tenant framework for observability metrics storage, as well as the project roadmap on what’s rolling out next for scalability and reliability.

Is the Agent in the Room with Us Right Now?

Speakers: Nick Rutigliano and Andrew Halaney, Netflix

Why I want to see it: At Netflix scale, moving traditional workloads to agentic AI requires understanding pros and cons of isolation. These requirements apply to security, RBAC network configurations, containers, and user namespaces, and general noisy neighbor improvements and can be useful takeaways for other end-users.

GenAI Observability: Keeping GenAI Honest Without Oversharing

Speaker: Liudmila Molkova, GrafanaLabs

Why I want to see it: Most end-users are leveraging Generative AI for their solutions and services. This talk will focus on how to instrument your AI applications with OpenTelemetry following the Generative AI Semantic Conventions. Understanding performance and usage telemetry data is important but understanding how to handle separate data for compliance and cost information is also valuable.

Tips for the best experience
  • Select topics and tracks that are most relevant for you. I’ve found this helps me short-list the sessions I’m most interested in
  • Plan for the time needed to get from one session to the next
  • Hallway discussions are very valuable 
  • Drop by the project pavilion. If you have a list of favorite projects, the project pavilion is a great place to meet the project maintainers in person
  • Have fun and enjoy learning from the conference sessions

Ben Somogyi

Enterprise Challenges with MCP Adoption

Speakers: Christian Posta (Global Field CTO at Solo.io)

Why I want to see it: In this session, Christion will be discussing the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and discussing some of the issues surrounding the establishment of MCP servers that provide access to enterprise APIs on behalf of a user.  Understanding how other companies are addressing these problems to prevent misuse and to protect their business through governance will be helpful to understand some best practices and strategies to leverage AI securely within your use case.

Navigating the Gateway API Maze: 40+ Implementations, 55+ Features, and a Path to Portability 

Speakers: Cristine Kim (Software Engineer, Isovalent at Cisco) and Beka Modebadze (Software Engineer @ Google)

Why I want to see it: In this talk Christine and Beka plan to “cut through the confusion and provide you with the knowledge to navigate the Gateway API landscape”.  In a dynamic space where capabilities like the nginx ingress controller have been recently retired this talk will help provide some insights of how to navigate the Gateway API space and discover implementations that will be portable and remain vendor agnostic, helping you build a more resilient platform with respect to k8s networking.

Kenta Tada

10 Years of Cilium: Connecting, Securing, and Simplifying the Cloud Native Stack

Speakers: Bill Mulligan and Paul Arah, Isovalent at Cisco; Marcelo Mello, Celonis; Neha Aggarwal, Microsoft

Why I want to see it: Cilium has become a crucial piece of software supporting modern container networking and security. In this session,  they will be sharing the latest updates alongside real-world stories from our users. It’s a great chance to see how it can simplify and secure your own infrastructure.

Ricardo Rocha

Gateway API: Bridging the Gap from Ingress to the Future

Speakers: Nick Young and James Strong, Isovalent at Cisco; Katarzyna Łach and Rostislav Bobrovsky, Google; Norwin Schnyder, Airlock

Why I want to see it: the transition from Ingress and services to the Gateway API is not trivial but it’s an opportunity for all end users to improve and simplify the ways they expose their endpoints. Listening from experts should bring a lot of good information. 

Virtual Power Plants (VPP): How They Work and What They Are

Speakers: LeRenzo Malcom and Mario Flores, Enpal

Why I want to see it: New use cases for cloud native usually make great talks. Managing power plants and solar devices as cloud native resources covers distributed, edge use cases and should bring useful information to all of us.

Instrumenting Kueue Scheduling for ML Training

Speakers: Amy Chen, CoreWeave and Gabriel Saba, Google

Why I want to see it: Kueue is an important tool for advanced scheduling and other capabilities needed for large scale, distributed ML training. A large end user’s feedback on their experiences along with one of the Kueue maintainers should make a great session.

The Shell Awakens: Cloud Native Workflows for Particle Physicists

Speakers: Raulian-Ionut Chiorescu and Hannes Hansen, CERN

Why I want to see it: This one comes from our team. Raul and Hannes will describe our next-gen scientific computing platform, and how we made the old and new ways of working compatible and used on top of the same resources and stack.

Tips for the best experience

  • Kubecon + CloudNativeCon has lots of sessions in a very large venue. Don’t overcommit the sessions you want to attend, consider the time to walk between rooms and leave time to ask questions to speakers after the sessions end
  • Spend time in the hallway, reach out to people, network as much as possible. These are the people that will help you out once back in the office, debugging or building new stacks. Also… check out the multiple parties happening every evening
  • Note down highlights and the names of those that can help you dig further later on. Bring it back to your teams, dedicate time to present and discuss your findings back home
  • Have fun! There is a lot of content and knowledge on display, but above all this event showcases and supports the community that keeps cloud native going strong!
】

在阿姆斯特丹举行的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon EU 2026 将云原生社区与项目、供应商和最终用户聚集在一起，这是最大的开源活动之一。

在这篇文章中，我们重点介绍最终用户可能特别感兴趣的会议和活动，包括专门的讨论论坛和 CNCF 最终用户技术咨询委员会 (TAB) 成员的建议，以及充分利用该活动的实用技巧。

最终用户内容

TAB 考虑了不同的方法来增强 KubeCon + CloudNative 最终用户的体验。这将是一个渐进的过程，但其中一些想法已经在阿姆斯特丹 Kubecon + CloudNativeCon Europe 之前得到实施。

确保您不会错过这些与其他最终用户讨论的机会。

羽毛之鸟 (BoF)

我们将举办三场针对最终用户的 BoF 会议，作为重点讨论我们如何针对特定主题并提出最佳实践和需要解决的差距的空间。对于本次 Kubecon + CloudNativeCon，我们将介绍：

  • BoF：人工智能基础设施和平台：法学硕士、代理人工智能
    • 召集人：约瑟夫·桑多瓦尔
    • 云原生平台上通用 MLOps 的最佳实践、在云原生基础设施上运行此类工作负载的常见痛点、现有生态系统中的差距
  • BoF：GPU/推理/训练/网络基础设施优化
    • 召集人：里卡多·罗查
    • 低级运行时优化、CPU/GPU NUMA 关联性和固定、低延迟网络、异构硬件支持
  • BoF：人工智能可观测性
    • 召集人：Alolita Sharma
    • 支持推理、训练和代理工作负载、当今生产中使用的工具以及现有生态系统中的差距的可观察性

市政厅

我们将设有通常的市政厅，最终用户可以向 TAB 提出问题。但这次我们改变了格式，以一系列 5 分钟的简短演示开始会议，这些演示应该会引发 TAB 和观众之间的讨论。

海报会议

我们将继续努力从最终用户那里发布更多参考架构，您可以在此处查看它们。我们为已发布架构的所有者提供了在海报会议展示他们自己的出版物。

路过可以直接提问。

最终用户接待

周二 17:30 – 18:30 将为 CNCF 最终用户会员举办招待会。欢迎前往地面层 Grand Cafe 咖啡厅 - 大楼 K 侧入口。

会议亮点

您可以在下面找到 TAB 成员提供的有关如何充分利用今年的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon 体验的会议建议和其他提示。

请随时联系我们，在走廊里聊天！

艾哈迈德·贝巴尔

1000 项服务，1 年，0 停机时间：Airbnb 的区域集群迁移

演讲者：Sunny Beattay，Airbnb

为什么我想看到它：我一直在寻找大型组织在没有读者可见的停机时间的情况下移动关键工作负载的真实故事。 Airbnb 讲述了他们如何在一年内以零停机时间将大约 1000 项服务迁移到区域拓扑。我对迁移策略、爆炸半径思维以及他们下次会采取哪些不同的做法特别感兴趣。

Kubernetes 尸检：实时调试集群崩溃 (Disney+ Hotstar)

演讲者：Sandeep Kanabar（Gen）、Aditi Gupta（Disney+ Hotstar）、Anshika Gupta（独立）

为什么我想看到它：新闻和媒体平台会经历流量峰值和类似于流媒体的硬 SLO。我很想知道 Disney+ Hotstar 如何应对真正的 Kubernetes 集群崩溃：他们的遥测显示（或未能显示）什么，他们如何在压力下进行调试，以及他们做出了哪些永久性改变来避免重蹈覆辙。

大规模智能路由：Spotify 的 XDS 控制平面如何减少 75% 的跨区流量

演讲者：Yannick Epstein、Anya Hristova – Spotify

为什么我想看到它：多可用区和多区域设置很容易通过不必要的跨区域流量泄漏资金。 Spotify 关于如何使用 XDS 控制平面将跨区域流量减少 75% 的演讲正是这种深入的技术路由和控制平面故事，可以为用户节省成本并改善延迟。

我们删除了可观测性堆栈并使用 OTEL 进行了重建：20K+ 集群中的 12 名工程师变为 4 名工程师 (DigitalOcean)

演讲者：Yash Sharma、Kunju Perath – DigitalOcean

为什么我想看到它：可观察性通常是成本、复杂性和辛劳悄悄积累的地方。 DigitalOcean 围绕 OpenTelemetry 为 20K+ 集群重建可观察性堆栈的故事，同时将团队从 12 人缩减到 4 人，听起来像专注于正确信号、重新思考架构以及平衡可见性与成本的大师班。

阿洛丽塔·夏尔马

黑暗中的碰撞：照亮你看不到的 95% Kubeflow

演讲者：Amine Lahouel 和 Laura Llinares，CERN

为什么我想看到它：这是一个有趣的会议，CERN 科学家将从可观察性的角度讨论他们对 ML 平台基础设施性能的需求。获得对整个系统的性能、资源消耗和整体健康状况的深入、可操作的洞察是最终用户的主要 KPI。了解 CERN 在使用这些 KPI 衡量 ML 平台性能方面的要点和挑战将非常有价值。

探索 Cortex：2026 年的高可扩展性指标

演讲者：Friedrich Gonzalez 和 Charlie Le，Apple

为什么我想看到它：本次会议旨在深入了解 Cortex（一个用于可观察性指标存储的多租户框架）的最新功能，以及下一步推出的可扩展性和可靠性的项目路线图。

特工现在在我们房间里吗？

演讲者：Nick Rutigliano 和 Andrew Halaney，Netflix

为什么我想看到它：在 Netflix 规模上，将传统工作负载转移到代理 AI 需要了解隔离的利弊。这些要求适用于安全性、RBAC 网络配置、容器和用户命名空间以及一般的嘈杂邻居改进，并且可以为其他最终用户带来有用的收获。

GenAI 可观察性：保持 GenAI 诚实而不过度分享

演讲者：Liudmila Molkova，GrafanaLabs

为什么我想看到它：大多数最终用户都在利用生成式 AI 来提供其解决方案和服务。本演讲将重点介绍如何按照生成式 AI 语义约定使用 OpenTelemetry 来检测您的 AI 应用程序。了解性能和使用情况遥测数据很重要，但了解如何处理合规性和成本信息的单独数据也很有价值。

最佳体验提示
  • 选择与您最相关的主题和曲目。我发现这可以帮助我列出我最感兴趣的会议
  • 计划从一个会话到下一个会话所需的时间
  • 走廊讨论非常有价值
  • 参观项目展馆。如果您有最喜欢的项目列表，项目展馆是与项目维护人员见面的好地方
  • 享受会议的乐趣并享受学习的乐趣

本·索莫吉

采用 MCP 所面临的企业挑战

演讲者：Christian Posta（Solo.io 全球现场首席技术官）

为什么我想看到它： 在本次会议中，Christion 将讨论模型上下文协议 (MCP) 并讨论围绕建立代表用户提供对企业 API 的访问的 MCP 服务器的一些问题。  了解其他公司如何解决这些问题以防止滥用并通过治理保护其业务，将有助于了解一些最佳实践和策略，以便在您的用例中安全地利用人工智能。

探索网关 API 迷宫：40 多种实现、55 多种功能和可移植性之路

演讲者：Cristine Kim（Cisco Isovalent 软件工程师）和 Beka Modebadze（Google 软件工程师）

为什么我想看到它：在这次演讲中，Christine 和 Beka 计划“消除困惑，为您提供驾驭 Gateway API 环境的知识”。  在诸如 nginx 入口控制器之类的功能最近已停用的动态空间中，本次演讲将有助于提供一些有关如何导航网关 API 空间的见解，并发现可移植且与供应商无关的实现，从而帮助您构建一个关于 k8s 网络的更具弹性的平台。

多田健太

Cilium 10 年：连接、保护和简化云原生堆栈

演讲者：Cisco Isovalent 的 Bill Mulligan 和 Paul Arah；马塞洛·梅洛、塞洛尼斯；内哈·阿加瓦尔，微软

为什么我想看到它：Cilium 已经成为支持现代容器网络和安全性的重要软件。在本次会议中，他们将分享最新动态以及我们用户的真实故事。这是了解它如何简化和保护您自己的基础设施的绝佳机会。

里卡多·罗查

网关 API：弥合 Ingress 与未来的差距

演讲者：思科 Isovalent 的 Nick Young 和 James Strong； Katarzyna Łach 和 Rostislav Bobrovsky，谷歌；诺文·施奈德，气闸

为什么我想看到它：从 Ingress 和服务到网关 API 的过渡并非微不足道，但对于所有最终用户来说，这是一个改进和简化他们公开端点的方式的机会。听取专家的意见应该会带来很多好的信息。 

虚拟发电厂 (VPP)：它们如何工作以及它们是什么

演讲者：LeRenzo Malcom 和 Mario Flores，恩帕尔

为什么我想看到它：clo的新用例ud本地人通常会发表精彩的演讲。将发电厂和太阳能设备作为云原生资源进行管理，涵盖分布式边缘用例，应该为我们所有人带来有用的信息。

为 ML 训练检测 Kueue 调度

演讲者：Amy Chen、CoreWeave 和 Gabriel Saba，Google

为什么我想看到它：Kueue 是高级调度和大规模分布式 ML 训练所需的其他功能的重要工具。大型最终用户与 Kueue 维护者之一对其体验的反馈应该会成为一次精彩的会议。

外壳觉醒：粒子物理学家的云原生工作流程

演讲者：Raulian-Ionut Chiorescu 和 Hannes Hansen，CERN

为什么我想看到它：这个来自我们的团队。 Raul 和 Hannes 将介绍我们的下一代科学计算平台，以及我们如何使新旧工作方式兼容并在相同的资源和堆栈上使用。

最佳体验提示

  • Kubecon + CloudNativeCon 在一个非常大的场地举办了很多会议。不要过多安排您想参加的会议，考虑在房间之间行走的时间，并在会议结束后留出时间向演讲者提问
  • 花时间在走廊里，与人接触，尽可能多地建立人际网络。这些人会在您回到办公室后帮助您调试或构建新堆栈。另外……看看每天晚上举行的多个派对
  • 记下重点内容以及有助于您日后进一步挖掘的内容名称。将其带回您的团队，花时间在家里展示和讨论您的发现
  • 玩得开心！展示了很多内容和知识，但最重要的是，这次活动展示并支持了使云原生保持强大的社区！

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: Platform Engineering Day】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深度探讨：平台工程日
item.description:
【

Platform Engineering Day launched at KubeCon in Paris in spring 2024 to create space for deeper discussion on platform engineering principles, practices, and real-world experiences. Now in its fifth edition in Amsterdam, the event reflects growing community demand as internal platforms become critical for accelerating software delivery and establishing consistent guardrails for emerging technologies such as AI and autonomous agents.

Attendees will gain insight into how organisations are building and operating cloud native platforms in production today, along with perspectives on where the field is heading. Shared lessons from practitioners will help teams avoid common pitfalls and make more informed architectural and organisational decisions.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

The program is designed to serve both newcomers to platform engineering and experienced practitioners operating platforms in production. Anyone exploring or actively working on internal platforms will find relevant technical and organisational insights.

What is new and different this year?

We have seen an increase in CFP submissions on the topics of Platform Engineering and AI, and we are starting to see more organisations able to speak about the practical application of AI and how it can deliver value. This year’s program includes more sessions focused on the real-world application of AI within platform engineering.

What will the day look like?

The day is heavily focused on platform engineering topics, but it also includes talks and panels focused on security—particularly how to build security and guardrails directly into platforms.

This year we will continue with the two-track format, allowing us to feature even more great content. We will start the day together with an update from the CNCF Platform Engineering Technical Community Group, followed by our keynote speakers.

After the morning break, we will split into two rooms featuring a range of sessions, including breakouts, panel discussions, and lightning talks. The program concludes with a joint closing session and an informal networking social.

Should I do any homework first?

No prep work necessary

Find your community!

The day begins by highlighting the work of the CNCF Platform Engineering Technical Community Group and inviting attendees to participate. This creates opportunities for contributors to share ideas, collaborate on initiatives, and help shape the direction of the platform engineering community.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

平台工程日于 2024 年春季在巴黎 KubeCon 上启动，旨在为更深入讨论平台工程原理、实践和实际经验创造空间。该活动现已在阿姆斯特丹举行第五届，反映出社区需求不断增长，因为内部平台对于加速软件交付和为人工智能和自主代理等新兴技术建立一致的护栏变得至关重要。

与会者将深入了解当今组织如何在生产中构建和运营云原生平台，以及对该领域发展方向的看法。从业者分享的经验教训将帮助团队避免常见的陷阱并做出更明智的架构和组织决策。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

该计划旨在为平台工程新手和生产中操作平台的经验丰富的从业者提供服务。任何探索或积极在内部平台上工作的人都会找到相关的技术和组织见解。

今年有什么新的和不同的？

我们看到有关平台工程和人工智能主题的 CFP 提交数量有所增加，并且我们开始看到更多组织能够谈论人工智能的实际应用及其如何创造价值。今年的计划包括更多专注于人工智能在平台工程中的实际应用的会议。

这一天会是什么样子？

这一天的重点是平台工程主题，但也包括专注于安全性的演讲和小组讨论，特别是如何将安全性和护栏直接构建到平台中。

今年我们将继续采用双轨格式，让我们能够展示更多精彩内容。我们将以 CNCF 平台工程技术社区组的最新消息开始新的一天，然后是我们的主旨演讲人。

早间休息后，我们将分成两个房间，举行一系列会议，包括分组讨论、小组讨论和闪电演讲。该计划以联合闭幕会议和非正式社交社交活动结束。

我应该先做作业吗？

无需准备工作

找到您的社区！

这一天首先重点介绍 CNCF 平台工程技术社区小组的工作并邀请与会者参与。这为贡献者创造了分享想法、协作开展计划并帮助塑造平台工程社区方向的机会。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【Policy-as-Code: Flexible Kubernetes governance with Kyverno】策略即代码：使用 Kyverno 进行灵活的 Kubernetes 治理
item.description:
【

Overview

Kubernetes has fundamentally transformed how enterprises deploy and manage business workloads. As organizations build production applications at scale on Kubernetes, cluster size and complexity continue to grow—creating unprecedented challenges in ensuring cluster security, compliance, and operational consistency.

Kubernetes natively enables developers to deploy workloads declaratively on demand, scale applications dynamically, and iterate rapidly. Unlike traditional IT governance models that rely on manual approval gates, Kubernetes DevSecOps processes must be automated and embedded directly into application development and delivery workflows to better realize the value of cloud native. Kubernetes also provides policy-based admission control mechanisms such as Admission Controllers and ValidatingAdmissionPolicy.

Policy governance frameworks provide built-in security controls based on industry-standard policy engines. This allows enterprise security and operations teams to implement common governance requirements in a codified and automated manner. Furthermore, predefined policy rule libraries are typically available to reduce the learning curve of policy languages and streamline development and operations workflows.

As Policy-as-Code becomes more widely adopted in production, a more flexible solution is needed to cover more complex scenarios and achieve a better balance between policy enforcement and developer productivity—Kyverno offers a Kubernetes-native approach to addressing these challenges.

Why Kyverno?

Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine. It uses standard CRDs to define and manage policies, providing a simpler and more user-friendly experience. Key benefits include:

  • Simple policy language: Policies are written in standard YAML, consistent with Kubernetes manifests. There is no need to learn specialized policy languages such as Rego, lowering the barrier to entry. Kyverno also extends the CEL function library, providing additional capabilities for complex policy expressions.
  • Rich feature set: Kyverno natively supports Validate, Mutate, Generate, and Cleanup, covering a broader range of automation and Policy-as-Code scenarios. It also provides robust caching mechanisms, external data calls, and other capabilities to meet enterprise extension needs in specific Policy-as-Code use cases.
  • Comprehensive policy library: Kyverno supports Kubernetes-native and multiple policy types, and the Kyverno community provides policy libraries covering many common Kubernetes application scenarios.
  • Standardized governance reporting and observability: While enforcing guardrails, enterprises often place even greater emphasis on the “visibility” of policy governance as a basis for continuously improving cluster security posture. Kyverno provides a Reporting mechanism aligned with the Kubernetes Policy Working Group specifications, helping audit whether workloads deployed in the cluster comply with standards and providing timely feedback to developers. Kyverno also integrates with standard cloud-native observability tools and can seamlessly integrate with cloud platform observability solutions.

To illustrate these capabilities, let’s explore how Kyverno addresses common production governance challenges.

Real-world use cases

Kyverno provides native support for Kubernetes and has been integrated by multiple cloud-native policy governance frameworks. It is now officially available for deployment on Kubernetes clusters through standard installation methods and platform marketplaces.

The following examples demonstrate how Kyverno addresses common production challenges.

Case 1: Enforcing Custom Resource (CRD) Deletion Protection

Driven by business needs, enterprises often deploy a large number of CRD resources in production clusters. Preventing accidental deletion of critical production CR instances is a key requirement for security and operations teams.

Within cloud-native policy governance frameworks, pre-built policy libraries typically cannot be aware of users’ custom resource definitions (CRDs). This limitation requires extending via external-provider mechanisms and also requires users to write and deploy specific policies to complete the workflow.

With Kyverno, this can be achieved using clear YAML expressions—friendly to cluster operators who are already familiar with Kubernetes manifests. Policies also support fine-grained resource matching and exemption mechanisms:

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: ValidatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: protect-critical-database-deletion
spec:
  autogen:
    podControllers:
      controllers: []
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['db.example.com']
        apiVersions: ['*']
        resources: ['databases']
        operations: ['DELETE']
  matchConditions:
    - name: critical-database-instances
      expression: "oldObject.metadata.name in ['critical-db', 'prod-main-db']"
  validationActions: ['Deny']
  validations:
    - expression: "oldObject.metadata.?annotations['kyverno.io/allow-delete'].orValue('') == 'true'"
      messageExpression: '''Deletion of critical database instance '' + oldObject.metadata.name + '' is not allowed unless annotated with kyverno.io/allow-delete: "true"'''

Case 2: Auto-mutating runAsNonRoot and generating network policies

In production operations, automatically applying related resource changes, adding labels/annotations, and dynamically hardening security for specific actions are common requirements. By using a policy engine together with Kubernetes mutating admission capabilities, enterprises can shift these processes from manual operations to automation.

Kyverno provides a more user-friendly mutation experience. Developers can perform resource mutations in a straightforward way using simple YAML patch operations. It also supports:

  • Covering existing resources: Scan, report on, mutate, and generate for existing resources in the cluster.
  • Automatic generation of related resources: Automatically create related resources such as NetworkPolicies and ConfigMaps based on policies to enable automated configuration.

The following is a typical example of automating a secure-by-default baseline. With the MutatingPolicy below, runAsNonRoot is automatically added to the Pod securityContext when Pods are created:

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: MutatingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-pod-run-as-non-root
spec:
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['pods']
  mutations:
    - patchType: ApplyConfiguration
      applyConfiguration:
        expression: >-
          Object{
            spec: Object.spec{
              securityContext: Object.spec.securityContext{
                runAsNonRoot: has(object.spec.securityContext) && has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot)
                             ? object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot
                             : true          
              }
            }
          }

The GeneratingPolicy below enables automatically creating namespace-scoped resources when a Namespace is created or updated:

apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
kind: GeneratingPolicy
metadata:
  name: add-networkpolicy
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Add Network Policy
    policies.kyverno.io/subject: NetworkPolicy
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.15.0
spec:
  evaluation:
    synchronize:
      enabled: true
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiGroups: ['']
        apiVersions: ['v1']
        operations: ['CREATE', 'UPDATE']
        resources: ['namespaces']
  variables:
    - name: targetNs
      expression: 'object.metadata.name'
    - name: downstream
      expression: >-
        [
          {
            "kind": dyn("NetworkPolicy"),
            "apiVersion": dyn("networking.k8s.io/v1"),
            "metadata": dyn({
              "name": "default-deny",
            }),
            "spec": dyn({
              "podSelector": dyn({}),
              "policyTypes": dyn(["Ingress", "Egress"])
            })
          }
        ]
  generate:
    - expression: generator.Apply(variables.targetNs, variables.downstream)

The Kyverno community’s policies project provides representative policies across a range of Kubernetes application scenarios and best practices. Interested readers can explore it to discover more possibilities with Kyverno.

Intelligent Policies × Security Governance Closed Loop

In the evolution of cloud-native security governance, Kyverno and AI Agents transcend a unidirectional toolchain—they form golden partners in constructing a dynamic “Perceive-Decide-Protect-Evolve” closed-loop system.

AI Agent Empowers Kyverno: From “Policy Configuration” to “Intelligent Governance”

Leveraging MCP/Skills integrated within the Agent and powered by LLM, SecOps teams can achieve one-click deployment of Kyverno alongside policy sets tailored for typical scenarios. The solution automatically scans workload configurations across defined cluster scopes for diverse compliance and baseline requirements, generating visualized security posture reports to precisely identify high-risk elements (e.g., privileged containers, sensitive volume mounts) and misconfigured authorizations. This transforms traditional “manual inspections” into “minute-level intelligent insights,” enabling security policy design with both foresight and precision.

Modern Kubernetes clusters support automated installation, deployment, and configuration capabilities for Kagent. Cluster security personnel can streamline the end-to-end lifecycle of building, deploying, and managing AI Agents within Kubernetes environments. Based on standardized MCP/Skills specifications, teams flexibly develop and operate Kyverno policy-focused Agents and MCP Servers directly in-cluster.

The figure below illustrates a Kyverno Agent instance deployed in the cluster. Integrated with Tools from kyverno-mcp, it supports context loading and switching across multi-cluster environments and enables policy governance inspection via the kyverno CLI for rapid security and compliance risk detection. Enhanced with Kyverno-specific Skills, the Agent optimizes policy governance workflows: as demonstrated in the interaction snapshot, it swiftly invokes Tools to evaluate namespace-level risks against best-practice policy sets, ultimately delivering tabular compliance reports and consolidated violation summaries.

Kyverno Safeguards AI Agents: From “Risk Prevention” to “Secure Operation”

When AI Agents, associated MCP Servers, and gateway containers are deployed within the cluster, Kyverno policies instantly step in as the vigilant "security gatekeeper":

  • Real-time interception of high-risk configurations: Automatically audit or reject Agent Pods launched with privileged mode, root privileges, missing securityContext, or mounting sensitive host directories.
  • Fine-grained AI gateway authorization: Leveraging Envoy’s External Authorization mechanism, Kyverno policies empower AI gateways to enforce identity-aware authorization, achieving fine-grained access control over AI inbound traffic.
  • Closed-loop hardening and remediation: Upon risk detection, Kyverno not only blocks non-compliant deployments but also delivers automated hardening and remediation templates—ensuring Agents operate as secure, governed application units within the cluster, not attack vectors.

In today’s era of deep AI and cloud-native convergence, Agents infuse Kyverno policies with contextual intelligence, while Kyverno empowers Agents with operational trust—together closing the loop on a dynamically evolving, self-reinforcing security framework.

Conclusion

In the cloud-native era, security and efficiency are no longer a binary trade-off. Kyverno brings policy governance closer to the Kubernetes user experience by expressing validation, mutation, generation, and cleanup logic in Kubernetes-native resources, enabling teams to enforce standards while keeping workflows simple and scalable.

Looking ahead, the Kyverno community will continue working with cloud vendors to advance interoperable governance capabilities across diverse environments. By co-building open policy libraries, standardized reporting and observability, and seamless integrations with vendor platforms and toolchains, organizations can establish consistent security and compliance baselines – making policy governance a shared, repeatable foundation for cloud-native operations.

】

概述

Kubernetes 从根本上改变了企业部署和管理业务工作负载的方式。随着组织在 Kubernetes 上大规模构建生产应用程序，集群规模和复杂性不断增长，这给确保集群安全性、合规性和操作一致性带来了前所未有的挑战。

Kubernetes 本身使开发人员能够按需以声明方式部署工作负载、动态扩展应用程序并快速迭代。与依赖手动审批门的传统 IT 治理模型不同，Kubernetes DevSecOps 流程必须自动化并直接嵌入到应用程序开发和交付工作流程中，以更好地实现云原生的价值。 Kubernetes 还提供基于策略的准入控制机制，例如准入控制器和 ValidatingAdmissionPolicy。

策略治理框架提供基于行业标准策略引擎的内置安全控制。这使得企业安全和运营团队能够以编码和自动化的方式实现共同的治理要求。此外，预定义的策略规则库通常可用于缩短策略语言的学习曲线并简化开发和运营工作流程。

随着策略即代码在生产中得到更广泛的采用，需要更灵活的解决方案来覆盖更复杂的场景，并在策略执行和开发人员生产力之间实现更好的平衡 - Kyverno 提供了 Kubernetes 原生方法来应对这些挑战。

为什么选择 Kyverno？

Kyverno 是 Kubernetes 原生策略引擎。它使用标准 CRD 来定义和管理策略，提供更简单、更人性化的体验。主要优点包括：

  • 简单的策略语言：策略以标准 YAML 编写，与 Kubernetes 清单一致。无需学习Rego等专门的政策语言，降低了准入门槛。 Kyverno 还扩展了 CEL 函数库，为复杂的策略表达式提供了额外的功能。
  • 丰富的功能集：Kyverno 本身支持验证、变异、生成和清理，涵盖更广泛的自动化和策略即代码场景。它还提供强大的缓存机制、外部数据调用和其他功能，以满足特定策略即代码用例中的企业扩展需求。
  • 全面的策略库：Kyverno 支持 Kubernetes 原生和多种策略类型，并且 Kyverno 社区提供了策略类型。com/kyverno/policies">策略库涵盖了许多常见的 Kubernetes 应用场景。
  • 标准化治理报告和可观察性：在实施防护措施的同时，企业往往更加重视政策治理的“可见性”，将其作为持续改善集群安全态势的基础。 Kyverno 提供了符合 Kubernetes 策略工作组规范的 Reporting 机制，帮助审核集群中部署的工作负载是否符合标准，并为开发人员提供及时的反馈。 Kyverno 还集成了标准的云原生可观测性工具，可以与云平台可观测性解决方案无缝集成。

为了说明这些功能，我们来探讨一下 Kyverno 如何解决常见的生产治理挑战。

现实世界用例

Kyverno 为 Kubernetes 提供原生支持，并已被多个云原生策略治理框架集成。它现已正式可以通过标准安装方法和平台市场部署在 Kubernetes 集群上。

以下示例演示了 Kyverno 如何解决常见的生产挑战。

案例 1：强制执行自定义资源 (CRD) 删除保护

受业务需求的驱动，企业往往会在生产集群中部署大量的CRD资源。防止意外删除关键生产 CR 实例是安全和运营团队的一项关键要求。

在云原生策略治理框架中，预构建的策略库通常无法了解用户的自定义资源定义 (CRD)。此限制需要通过外部提供商机制进行扩展，并且还需要用户编写和部署特定策略来完成工作流程。

借助 Kyverno，可以使用清晰的 YAML 表达式来实现这一点，这对集群操作员来说很友好已经熟悉 Kubernetes 清单。政策还支持细粒度的资源匹配和豁免机制：

api版本：policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
种类：验证策略
元数据：
  名称：保护关键数据库删除
规格：
  自动生成：
    pod 控制器：
      控制器：[]
  匹配限制：
    资源规则：
      - apiGroups: ['db.example.com']
        api版本：['*']
        资源：['数据库']
        操作：['删除']
  匹配条件：
    - 名称：关键数据库实例
      表达式：“['ritic-db', 'prod-main-db'] 中的 oldObject.metadata.name”
  验证操作：['拒绝']
  验证：
    - 表达式：“oldObject.metadata.?annotations['kyverno.io/allow-delete'].orValue('') == 'true'”
      messageExpression: '''删除关键数据库实例 '' + oldObject.metadata.name + '' 是不允许的，除非用 kyverno.io/allow-delete: "true"''' 注释

案例 2：自动修改 runAsNonRoot 并生成网络策略

在生产操作中，自动应用相关资源更改、添加标签/注释以及动态强化特定操作的安全性是常见需求。通过使用策略引擎和 Kubernetes 变异准入功能，企业可以将这些流程从手动操作转变为自动化。

Kyverno 提供了更加用户友好的突变体验。开发人员可以使用简单的 YAML 修补操作以直接的方式执行资源变更。它还支持：

  • 覆盖现有资源：扫描、报告、变异和生成集群中的现有资源。
  • 自动生成相关资源：根据策略自动创建 NetworkPolicies、ConfigMap 等相关资源，实现自动化配置。

以下是自动化默认安全基线的典型示例。使用下面的 MutatingPolicy，在创建 Pod 时，runAsNonRoot 会自动添加到 Pod securityContext 中：

api版本：policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
种类：变异策略
元数据：
  名称：add-pod-run-as-non-root
规格：
  匹配限制：
    资源规则：
      - api 组：['']
        api版本：['v1']
        操作：['创建'，'更新']
        资源：['豆荚']
  突变：
    - 补丁类型：应用配置
      应用配置：
        表达方式：>-
          对象{
            规格：对象.规格{
              securityContext: Object.spec.securityContext{
                runAsNonRoot: has(object.spec.securityContext) && has(object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot)
                             ？ object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot：真实          
              }
            }
          }

下面的 GeneatingPolicy 可以在创建或更新命名空间时自动创建命名空间范围内的资源：

api版本：policies.kyverno.io/v1alpha1
种类：生成策略
元数据：
  名称：添加网络策略
  注释：
    policies.kyverno.io/title：添加网络策略
    policy.kyverno.io/subject：网络策略
    policies.kyverno.io/minversion：1.15.0
规格：
  评价：
    同步：
      启用：真
  匹配限制：
    资源规则：
      - api 组：['']
        api版本：['v1']
        操作：['创建'，'更新']
        资源：['命名空间']
  变量：
    - 名称：目标Ns
      表达式：'对象.元数据.名称'
    - 名称：下游
      表达方式：>-
        [
          {
            “种类”：dyn（“网络策略”），
            "apiVersion": dyn("networking.k8s.io/v1"),
            “元数据”：dyn（{
              "name": "默认拒绝",
            }),
            “规格”：动态（{
              “podSelector”：dyn（{}），
              "policyTypes": dyn(["入口", "出口"])
            })
          }
        ]
  生成：
    - 表达式：generator.Apply(variables.targetNs,variables.downstream)

Kyverno 社区的策略项目提供了跨一系列 Kubernetes 应用场景和最佳实践的代表性策略。有兴趣的读者可以探索一下，发现 Kyverno 的更多可能性。

智能策略×安全治理闭环

在云原生安全治理的演进过程中，Kyverno 和 AI Agent 超越了单向工具链，成为构建动态“感知-决策-保护-演进”闭环系统的黄金搭档。

AI Agent赋能Kyverno：从“策略配置”到“智能治理”

利用代理内集成的 MCP/技能并由 LLM 提供支持，SecOps 团队可以实现 Kyverno 的一键部署以及针对典型场景定制的策略集。该解决方案自动扫描定义的集群范围内的工作负载配置，以满足不同的合规性和基线要求，生成可视化的安全态势报告，以精确识别高风险元素（例如特权容器、敏感卷安装）和错误配置的授权。这将传统的“人工检查”转变为“分钟级的智能洞察”，使安全策略设计具有前瞻性和精准性。

现代 Kubernetes 集群支持自动化安装、部署和配置功能 Kagent。集群安全人员可以简化 Kubernetes 环境中构建、部署和管理 AI Agent 的端到端生命周期。基于标准化的 MCP/Skills 规范，团队可以直接在集群内灵活开发和运营以策略为中心的 Kyverno Agent 和 MCP 服务器。

下图展示了集群中部署的 Kyverno Agent 实例。它与 kyverno-mcp 的工具集成，支持跨多集群环境的上下文加载和切换，并通过 kyverno CLI 启用策略治理检查，以实现快速安全和合规风险检测。该代理通过 Kyverno 特定技能进行增强，优化了策略治理工作流程：如交互快照所示，它可以快速调用工具来根据最佳实践策略集评估命名空间级风险，最终提供表格合规性报告和综合违规摘要。

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: CiliumCon】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深入探讨：CiliumCon
item.description:
【

Three years later, CiliumCon is back where it started—at KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe in Amsterdam. This will be our seventh time hosting the event, and it comes on the heels of the tenth anniversary of Cilium’s first commit. Over the past decade, the project has evolved from an experimental container networking project to the de facto CNI for the cloud native ecosystem.

This event comes as the project finds its direction for the next decade, positioning itself as the networking data plane for AI workloads, while Tetragon is poised to redefine the runtime security landscape.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

Platform engineers and infrastructure teams running Kubernetes in production will get the most out of CiliumCon—particularly those managing multi-cluster environments, dealing with security policy at scale, or evaluating Cilium as a replacement for legacy networking hardware or another CNI.

CiliumCon 2026 also has a strong security thread running through it, from Tetragon-based per-workload policy enforcement to hardware-accelerated security policies on DPUs. 

What is new and different this year?

The agenda reflects a maturation of the ecosystem, with talks moving beyond “how do we adopt Cilium?” to “how do we run it at scale using advanced features?” Topics include scaling Tetragon policies, multi-cluster networking across hundreds of clusters, and replacing legacy hardware load balancers.

The event also lands shortly after the latest release of Cilium (v1.19), with several sessions touching on its newest features and upgrades. Microsoft will walk through flow aggregation—one of 1.19’s headline observability features—while Roche will discuss expanded DNS policy wildcard support.

What will the day look like?

The Cilium and eBPF community will come together on the afternoon of Monday, March 23, kicking off with opening remarks from co-chairs Mahé Tardy and Hemanth Malla. There will be five technical sessions from project maintainers and end users, along with three lightning talks highlighting new features and advanced setups.

We’ll also hear a keynote from Bill Mulligan highlighting why Cisco is betting big on Cilium and eBPF, and how Tetragon is redefining networking, security, and observability in the multi-cloud world.

Should I do any homework first?

Whether you’re a seasoned practitioner or just getting started, a basic understanding of Kubernetes networking and container orchestration will help you get the most out of the sessions.

If you’re new to eBPF, some great resources include the eBPF documentary for the full backstory on why this technology is a game-changer, the children’s guide to eBPF, Buzzing Across Space, for a fun introduction, and Liz Rice’s Learning eBPF for a comprehensive guide.

For those planning to follow the Tetragon and multi-cluster sessions closely, some familiarity with eBPF-based security tooling and service mesh concepts will be useful—but it shouldn’t be a barrier. If you want to get hands-on before the event, be sure to check out the Cilium labs or read through Cilium: Up and Running.

Find your community!

CiliumCon provides a dedicated space for the Cilium community to come together outside of the broader KubeCon noise, bringing end users, contributors, and maintainers into the same room to share what’s actually working in production.

End-user talks from organizations like Roche, Etraveli Group, Ledger, and SUSE reinforce that this is a real-world, battle-tested project. Talks from contributors and maintainers help community members stay current with fast-moving developments.

Together, the event helps new adopters learn from experienced practitioners and gives contributors direct feedback on what the community needs most.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

三年后，CiliumCon 回到了起点——阿姆斯特丹的 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe。这将是我们第七次举办该活动，紧接着是 Cilium 首次提交十周年。在过去的十年里，该项目已经从一个实验性的容器网络项目发展成为云原生生态系统事实上的 CNI。

此次活动恰逢该项目找到未来十年的方向，将自己定位为人工智能工作负载的网络数据平面，而 Tetragon 则准备重新定义运行时安全格局。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

在生产中运行 Kubernetes 的平台工程师和基础设施团队将充分利用 CiliumCon，特别是那些管理多集群环境、大规模处理安全策略或评估 Cilium 作为传统网络硬件或其他 CNI 替代品的工程师和基础设施团队。

CiliumCon 2026 还拥有贯穿其中的强大安全线程，从基于 Tetragon 的按工作负载策略实施到 DPU 上的硬件加速安全策略。 

今年有什么新的和不同的？

议程反映了生态系统的成熟，讨论超出了“我们如何采用 Cilium？”的范围。到“我们如何使用高级功能大规模运行它？”主题包括扩展 Tetragon 策略、跨数百个集群的多集群网络以及替换传统硬件负载均衡器。

该活动也在 Cilium (v1.19) 最新版本发布后不久举行，其中有几场会议涉及其最新功能和升级。 Microsoft 将介绍流量聚合（1.19 的主要可观察性功能之一），而 Roche 将讨论扩展的 DNS 策略通配符支持。

这一天会是什么样子？

Cilium 和 eBPF 社区将于 3 月 23 日星期一下午齐聚一堂，由联合主席 Mahé Tardy 和 Hemanth Malla 致开幕词。将有五项技术来自项目维护人员和最终用户的校准会议，以及三个强调新功能和高级设置的闪电演讲。

我们还将听到 Bill Mulligan 的主题演讲，强调思科为何在 Cilium 和 eBPF 上投入巨资，以及 Tetragon 如何重新定义多云世界中的网络、安全性和可观察性。

我应该先做作业吗？

无论您是经验丰富的从业者还是刚刚入门，对 Kubernetes 网络和容器编排的基本了解都将帮助您从课程中获得最大收益。

如果您是 eBPF 的新手，一些很棒的资源包括 eBPF 纪录片，其中提供了有关为何这项技术能够改变游戏规则的完整背景故事、eBPF 儿童指南、Buzzing Across Space（提供了有趣的介绍）以及 Liz Rice 的学习 eBPF（提供了全面的指南）。

对于那些计划密切关注 Tetragon 和多集群会议的人来说，熟悉基于 eBPF 的安全工具和服务网格概念将会很有用，但这不应该成为障碍。如果您想在活动前亲身实践，请务必查看 Cilium 实验室或通读Cilium：启动并运行。

找到您的社区！

CiliumCon 为 Cilium 社区提供了一个专门的空间，让他们能够在更广泛的 KubeCon 噪音之外聚集在一起，将最终用户、贡献者和维护者带到同一个房间来分享生产中实际运行的内容。

来自 Roche、Etraveli Group、Ledger 和 SUSE 等组织的最终用户谈话强化了这是一个真实的、经过实战考验的项目。贡献者和维护者的演讲可帮助社区成员跟上快速发展的步伐。

该活动帮助新采用者向经验丰富的从业者学习，并为贡献者提供关于社区最需要什么的直接反馈。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【Understanding Kubernetes metrics: Best practices for effective monitoring】了解 Kubernetes 指标：有效监控的最佳实践
item.description:
【

Kubernetes metrics show cluster activity. You need them to manage Kubernetes clusters, nodes, and applications. Without them, it also makes it harder to find problems and improve performance.

This post will explain what Kubernetes metrics are, the various kinds you should be aware of, how to gather them.

What are Kubernetes metrics?

Kubernetes metrics are pieces of information that indicate how well the items operating in your Kubernetes environment are performing. They are vital because it’s hard to find problems or solve them before they harm your apps without them. They show you how the cluster is performing.

Types of Kubernetes metrics

Kubernetes generates different metrics that help you understand performance at different system layers. Here are the common ones:

Cluster metrics

In Kubernetes, a cluster refers to the complete environment that executes your application. It has the control plane (API server, scheduler, controller manager, etc.), nodes (VMs or physical servers), and pods/workloads (the containers that operate your program).

So cluster metrics are a summary of metrics from the control plane, nodes, and pods/containers. They provide data relating to the health, performance, and resource usage of the whole Kubernetes cluster.

1. Node CPU resource usage

This tells you how much CPU capacity the cluster’s nodes are consuming compared to what is available. You can determine the total CPU usage of the cluster by summing the CPU consumption of all the nodes. Each node has a set amount of CPU power. You can tell if nodes are overloaded, balanced, or underused by keeping an eye on this metric.

2. Node memory usage

This indicates how much memory (RAM) all the processes on nodes are using, such as kubelet, container runtimes, running pods, and system services. Some important components of memory usage include:

  • Working set memory: Memory actively in use by processes that cannot be reclaimed.
  • Cache memory: File system cache that can be reclaimed if needed
  • RSS (Resident Set Size): Portion of memory occupied in RAM.

Monitoring node memory usage helps prevent clusters from running out of resources, which can result in the termination and removal of a pod from a node or the crashing of apps.

3. Node disk usage

When disk use goes over a certain point, like when there is less than 10–15% free space, the kubelet kicks out a pod to make room.

Node disk usage indicates the amount of space each node in a cluster occupies on the hard drive. There may be no pods left on the node if you run out of disk space.

Node metrics

In Kubernetes clusters, node metrics provide lots of information regarding node health and resource usage.

Monitoring individual nodes helps troubleshoot issues on specific nodes. Metrics like Node CPU Usage and Node Memory Consumption show how much CPU and memory a node is using.

Other node metrics include:

1. Disk I/O and available disk space

Disk I/O measures the read/write rate on a node’s storage devices, while Available Disk Space shows the remaining free space on the node’s filesystem.

High disk I/O can slow down workloads that perform heavy read/write tasks, causing latency. Also, running out of disk space may trigger pod eviction or node failures.

2. Network bandwidth usage

This shows the data sent and received by a node’s network interfaces per second. Network issues can impact communication between pods, nodes, and external services, causing delays or timeouts.

Control plane metrics

Control plane metrics indicate the effectiveness of the control plane. Just for clarity, Kubernetes has two main parts:

  • Control plane: The brain (decides what should run where)
  • Nodes: The worker (actually runs your apps and containers).

The control plane is where Kubernetes holds its decision-making and cluster-running capabilities. It schedules your programs, or pods, and monitors the cluster’s health. It also provides instructions to the worker nodes.

Tracking control plane metrics helps detect issues with key services like the API server, scheduler, and controllers, ensuring cluster reliability and responsiveness.

1. API server request latencies

This is how long the Kubernetes API server takes to respond to client queries like listing nodes, deploying items, and getting pod information. It shows how long messages and replies take.

Assume that a request to list every pod typically takes 100 milliseconds. The server may be suffering a delay as a result of an overload if there is a delay of up to two seconds.

An excessively long API request indicates a sluggish control plane, which may make the entire cluster less responsive.

2. Scheduler queue length

Shows the number of pods waiting to be scheduled to a node. Monitoring queue length helps detect resource shortages or scheduler performance issues early.

The cluster may be experiencing issues with resource allocation or setup if the scheduler queue length, which is typically small, unexpectedly increases to 50+.

The availability and user experience can be negatively affected by delays in app deployment or workload scalability caused by a large queue length.

Pod metrics

Pod metrics are the performance data about your running pods. Kubernetes Monitoring helps you catch problems early, know when to scale your apps, and keep everything running smoothly before users notice any issues.

Aside from CPU usage and memory consumption, here are other examples of pod metrics.

1. Pod Restart Count

This displays how many times a pod’s container restarted due to a crash, failure, or resource shortage. If the system restarts often, apps or resources might be the problem.

2. Pending Pod Count

This shows how many pods are pending. Pending pods are the ones that are still waiting to be scheduled to a node. They wait for the Kubernetes scheduler to locate a node that is good for them.

Pods can be pending for the following reasons:

  • Insufficient resources available
  • Scheduling problems
  • Problems with image

Run “kubectl get pods — field-selector=status.phase=Pending” to get a list of pods to be scheduled.

3. Pod Status

Pod status shows the current state or health of a pod and indicates if it’s running smoothly or facing any challenges. Common pod statuses include:

  • Pending: The pod has been accepted by Kubernetes, but its node operation has not yet been scheduled.
  • Running: Pod is assigned a node, and at least one container is running.
  • Succeeded: All containers in the pod completed successfully.
  • Failed: One or more containers terminated with an error.
  • Unknown: Kubernetes is unable to ascertain the pod’s condition as a result of poor communication.

If you run the command:

kubectl get pods

You’ll see the list of pods and their status.

How to collect metrics in Kubernetes

You need Kubernetes monitoring tools that collect, process, and occasionally store information for analysis because Kubernetes does not store all metrics by default.

Metrics server

Metrics Server collects CPU and memory metrics for nodes and pods in Kubernetes. It provides real-time resource usage data for other components instead of storing it long-term.

Metrics Server collects resource usage data from the kubelet on each node and exposes aggregated metrics through the Kubernetes API. It gathers data every 15 seconds to provide near real-time resource utilization.

Here’s how to set up Metrics Server:

1. Install the Metric Server using the kubectl command

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

This will create the necessary Deployment, ServiceAccount, roles, and bindings.

2. Verify installation by running the command “kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system”

This will ensure it is running with available pods.

3. Then you can view metrics for both nodes and pods.

For nodes:

For pods (across all namespaces):

cAdvisor

Container Advisor, or cAdvisor, gathers data on resource consumption in real time for pods and containers.

Since cAdvisor is embedded within the kubelet, it automatically collects container-level data on every node. The Kubelet’s endpoint provides metrics that may be used with Prometheus to create dashboards and analyze data.

Kube-State-Metrics

This tool monitors Kubernetes nodes, namespaces, deployments, and pods. It tracks cluster state, configuration, and health, enabling monitoring systems to collect metrics for visualization, alerting, and analysis.

Here’s how to install it:

1. Install via Helm

First, add the Prometheus community Help repo:

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

Install Kube-State-Metrics in your cluster:

helm install kube-state-metrics prometheus-community/kube-state-metrics


This creates the necessary deployments, service accounts, and permissions automatically.

2. Install manually using YAML manifests

Apply the official Kube-State-Metrics manifests provided in the GitHub repo:

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes/kube-state-metrics/releases/latest/download/kube-state-metrics-standalone-release.yaml


This will deploy Kube-State-Metrics in your cluster. You can go ahead to confirm if it’s running:

Conclusion

Metrics help you check pods, nodes, and clusters. They allow you to keep an eye on the health and condition of workloads to find problems early and make sure apps work well.

To understand the context of what problems metrics can reveal, explore our blog on Kubernetes Troubleshooting Techniques.

Conclusion

Metrics help you check pods, nodes, and clusters. They allow you to keep an eye on the health and condition of workloads to find problems early and make sure apps work well.

To understand the context of what problems metrics can reveal, explore our blog on Kubernetes Troubleshooting Techniques.

This Member Blog was originally published on the Middleware blog and is republished here with permission.

】

Kubernetes 指标显示集群活动。您需要它们来管理 Kubernetes 集群、节点和应用程序。如果没有它们，发现问题和提高性能也会变得更加困难。

这篇文章将解释什么是 Kubernetes 指标、您应该了解的各种指标以及如何收集它们。

什么是 Kubernetes 指标？

Kubernetes 指标是指示 Kubernetes 环境中运行的项目执行情况的信息。它们至关重要，因为如果没有它们，就很难在问题损害您的应用程序之前找到或解决它们。它们向您展示集群的性能。

Kubernetes 指标类型

Kubernetes 生成不同的指标，帮助您了解不同系统层的性能。以下是常见的：

集群指标

在 Kubernetes 中，集群是指执行应用程序的完整环境。它具有控制平面（API 服务器、调度程序、控制器管理器等）、节点（虚拟机或物理服务器）和 Pod/工作负载（运行程序的容器）。

因此，集群指标是来自控制平面、节点和 Pod/容器的指标的汇总。它们提供与整个 Kubernetes 集群的运行状况、性能和资源使用情况相关的数据。

1.节点CPU资源使用情况

这会告诉您与可用容量相比，集群节点消耗了多少 CPU 容量。通过对所有节点的CPU消耗进行求和，可以确定集群的总CPU使用率。每个节点都有一定数量的 CPU 功率。您可以通过密切关注此指标来判断节点是否过载、平衡或未充分利用。

2.节点内存使用情况

这表示节点上的所有进程正在使用多少内存 (RAM)，例如 kubelet、容器运行时、运行的 Pod 和系统服务。内存使用的一些重要组成部分包括：

  • 工作集内存：进程正在使用且无法回收的内存。
  • 缓存内存：需要时可以回收的文件系统缓存
  • RSS（Resident Set Size）：RAM 中占用的内存部分。

监控节点内存使用情况有助于防止集群耗尽资源，这可能会导致 Pod 终止并从节点中删除或应用程序崩溃。

3.节点磁盘使用情况

当磁盘使用超过某个点时，例如可用空间少于 10-15% 时，kubelet 会踢出一个 pod 来腾出空间。

节点磁盘使用率表示集群中每个节点在硬盘上占用的空间量。如果磁盘空间不足，节点上可能没有剩余 pod。

遇到节点参考文献

在 Kubernetes 集群中，节点指标提供了大量有关节点运行状况和资源使用情况的信息。

监控各个节点有助于解决特定节点上的问题。节点 CPU 使用率和节点内存消耗等指标显示节点使用了多少 CPU 和内存。

其他节点指标包括：

1.磁盘 I/O 和可用磁盘空间

磁盘 I/O 衡量节点存储设备上的读/写速率，而可用磁盘空间则显示节点文件系统上的剩余可用空间。

高磁盘 I/O 会减慢执行繁重读/写任务的工作负载，从而导致延迟。此外，磁盘空间不足可能会触发 Pod 驱逐或节点故障。

2.网络带宽使用情况

这显示了节点网络接口每秒发送和接收的数据。网络问题可能会影响 Pod、节点和外部服务之间的通信，导致延迟或超时。

控制平面指标

控制平面指标表明控制平面的有效性。为清楚起见，Kubernetes 有两个主要部分：

  • 控制平面：大脑（决定什么应该在哪里运行）
  • 节点：工作线程（实际运行您的应用和容器）。

控制平面是 Kubernetes 掌握决策和集群运行能力的地方。它安排您的程序或 Pod，并监控集群的运行状况。它还向工作节点提供指令。

跟踪控制平面指标有助于检测 API 服务器、调度程序和控制器等关键服务的问题，从而确保集群的可靠性和响应能力。

1. API服务器请求延迟

这是 Kubernetes API 服务器响应客户端查询（例如列出节点、部署项目和获取 Pod 信息）所需的时间。它显示消息和回复需要多长时间。

假设列出每个 Pod 的请求通常需要 100 毫秒。如果延迟长达两秒，服务器可能会因过载而出现延迟。

过长的 API 请求表明控制平面缓慢，这可能会降低整个集群的响应速度。

2.调度程序队列长度

显示等待调度到节点的 Pod 数量。监控队列长度有助于及早发现资源短缺或调度程序性能问题。

如果调度程序队列长度（通常很小）意外增加到 50 以上，则集群可能会遇到资源分配或设置问题。

队列长度过大导致的应用部署延迟或工作负载可扩展性可能会对可用性和用户体验产生负面影响。

Pod 指标

Pod 指标是 p有关跑步 Pod 的性能数据。 Kubernetes 监控可帮助您及早发现问题，了解何时扩展应用程序，并在用户注意到任何问题之前保持一切顺利运行。

除了 CPU 使用率和内存消耗之外，以下是 Pod 指标的其他示例。

1. Pod 重启计数

这显示 Pod 的容器由于崩溃、故障或资源短缺而重新启动的次数。如果系统经常重新启动，则可能是应用程序或资源出现问题。

2.待处理 Pod 计数

这显示有多少个 Pod 处于待处理状态。待处理的 pod 是仍在等待调度到节点的 pod。他们等待 Kubernetes 调度程序找到适合他们的节点。

Pod 可能因以下原因而处于待处理状态：

  • 可用资源不足
  • 日程安排问题
  • 图像问题

运行“kubectl get pods — field-selector=status.phase=Pending”以获取要调度的 Pod 列表。

3. Pod 状态

Pod 状态显示 Pod 的当前状态或运行状况，并指示它是否运行顺利或面临任何挑战。常见的 Pod 状态包括：

  • 待处理：该 Pod 已被 Kubernetes 接受，但其节点操作尚未安排。
  • 运行中：Pod 被分配一个节点，并且至少有一个容器正在运行。
  • 成功：容器中的所有容器均已成功完成。
  • 失败：一个或多个容器因错误而终止。
  • 未知：由于通信不畅，Kubernetes 无法确定 Pod 的状况。

如果运行命令：

kubectl 获取 pod

您将看到 Pod 列表及其状态。

如何在 Kubernetes 中收集指标

您需要 Kubernetes 监控收集、处理和偶尔存储信息以进行分析的工具，因为 Kubernetes 默认情况下并不存储所有指标。

指标服务器

Metrics Server 收集 Kubernetes 中节点和 Pod 的 CPU 和内存指标。它为其他组件提供实时资源使用数据，而不是长期存储。

Metrics Server 从每个节点上的 kubelet 收集资源使用数据，并通过 Kubernetes API 公开聚合指标。它每 15 秒收集一次数据，以提供近乎实时的资源利用率。

以下是设置 Metrics Server 的方法：

<强>1。使用 kubectl 命令安装 Metric Server

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml

这将创建必要的 Deployment、ServiceAccount、角色和绑定。

2.通过运行命令“kubectl get部署指标-server -n kube-system”

来验证安装

这将确保它使用可用的 Pod 运行。

3.然后您可以查看节点和 Pod 的指标。

对于节点：

item.title: 【KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 Co-located Event Deep Dive: KyvernoCon】KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026 同期举办活动深入探讨：KyvernoCon
item.description:
【

As Kubernetes adoption continues to accelerate across enterprise platforms and AI-driven workloads, organizations increasingly need automated guardrails for security, governance, and compliance.

KyvernoCon launched in 2025 as the first event dedicated to the Kyverno community and the growing role of policy as code in Kubernetes environments. As Kyverno sees rapid adoption, a growing contributor community, and expanding real-world use across the ecosystem, this event creates an opportunity for practitioners to share lessons learned and explore how policy-driven approaches are shaping modern platform engineering in the era of AI.

Who will get the most out of attending this event?

KyvernoCon is valuable for anyone building, operating, or securing Kubernetes platforms. Platform engineers, SREs, and security practitioners running Kubernetes in production will gain practical insights into implementing policy-driven governance, security guardrails, and operational consistency at scale.

Our event is also valuable for open source contributors, ecosystem partners, and practitioners who want to better understand how policy as code fits into modern cloud native platforms and connect with others working on similar challenges across the global community.

Platform Engineering
Policy as code is increasingly becoming a core capability for platform teams operating Kubernetes environments at scale. Kyverno enables platform engineers to embed governance, security, and operational guardrails directly into their platforms while improving consistency and the developer experience.

Security
Kyverno helps teams automate security and compliance policies directly within Kubernetes workflows, allowing organizations to enforce configuration standards, reduce risk, and address supply chain and runtime security concerns earlier in the software delivery lifecycle.

What is new and different this year?

This year’s event reflects the rapid evolution of the policy-as-code landscape as Kubernetes adoption continues to expand across increasingly complex environments. Recent Kyverno releases have introduced stronger alignment with emerging Kubernetes policy standards, including expanded CEL-based policy capabilities and improvements that support policy-driven workflows across development and runtime environments.

At the same time, the Kyverno community has grown significantly, with more organizations adopting policy-driven approaches to manage security, governance, and operational guardrails at scale. KyvernoCon provides a forum for practitioners to share production experiences, ecosystem integrations, and lessons learned as policy-as-code becomes a foundational capability for modern platform engineering.

What will the day look like?

KyvernoCon is a half-day, community-driven event featuring technical talks, real-world case studies, demos, and interactive discussions. Sessions highlight how organizations are applying policy-as-code to manage governance, security, and operational guardrails across Kubernetes environments.

The event also creates space for conversation and collaboration among maintainers, contributors, and practitioners, giving attendees opportunities to exchange ideas, ask questions, and connect with others working on similar challenges across the cloud native ecosystem.

Attendees should leave with a clearer understanding of how policy as code can be applied across the Kubernetes lifecycle, from development pipelines to production clusters, to automate governance and improve security, reliability, and operational consistency.

Through real-world case studies and technical sessions, participants will see how organizations are implementing policy-driven approaches to address challenges such as compliance, software supply chain security, and multi-cluster platform management. Our event also offers perspective on how policy is becoming an integral layer in cloud native platforms as teams look to scale Kubernetes safely and efficiently in the era of AI.

Should I do any homework first?

No extensive preparation is required, but a basic familiarity with Kubernetes concepts such as resources, admission control, or configuration management can help attendees get more out of the discussions.

For those who want additional context beforehand, reviewing Kyverno documentation or experimenting with a simple policy can provide helpful background and make the technical sessions easier to follow, although we’re proud to offer a balanced schedule of talks that span all knowledge levels. Additionally, we will host a ContribFest session later in the week where additional questions and support—from beginners to advanced users—will be welcomed. Please consider joining us there after KyvernoCon to continue the discussion!

Find your community!

KyvernoCon creates a dedicated space for users, contributors, and maintainers to connect, share experiences, and collaborate on the evolving practice of policy as code. As Kyverno adoption continues to grow across organizations and industries, bringing the community together helps surface real-world lessons, strengthen feedback loops between users and maintainers, and accelerate shared learning across the ecosystem.

Events like this also make it easier for new contributors and practitioners to engage with the project, discover opportunities to participate, and connect with others working on similar challenges. By fostering open discussion, knowledge sharing, and collaboration, KyvernoCon supports the continued growth and long-term sustainability of our global Kyverno community as it continues to mature within the cloud native ecosystem.

Don’t forget to register for KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026.

】

随着 Kubernetes 在企业平台和人工智能驱动的工作负载中的采用不断加速，组织越来越需要自动化护栏来实现安全、治理和合规性。

KyvernoCon 于 2025 年推出，是第一个专门针对 Kyverno 社区以及政策即代码在 Kubernetes 环境中日益重要的作用的活动。随着 Kyverno 的快速采用、不断增长的贡献者社区以及整个生态系统在现实世界中的使用不断扩大，本次活动为从业者创造了一个机会，分享经验教训并探索政策驱动的方法如何塑造人工智能时代的现代平台工程。

谁将从参加本次活动中获益最多？

KyvernoCon 对于任何构建、操作或保护 Kubernetes 平台的人都很有价值。在生产中运行 Kubernetes 的平台工程师、SRE 和安全从业者将获得有关大规模实施策略驱动的治理、安全护栏和操作一致性的实用见解。

我们的活动对于开源贡献者、生态系统合作伙伴和从业者来说也很有价值，他们希望更好地了解政策即代码如何适应现代云原生平台，并与全球社区中致力于解决类似挑战的其他人建立联系。

平台工程
策略即代码正日益成为大规模运营 Kubernetes 环境的平台团队的核心能力。 Kyverno 使平台工程师能够将治理、安全和操作护栏直接嵌入到他们的平台中，同时提高一致性和开发人员体验。

安全性
Kyverno 帮助团队直接在 Kubernetes 工作流程中自动执行安全性和合规性策略，从而使组织能够在软件交付生命周期的早期实施配置标准、降低风险并解决供应链和运行时安全问题。

今年有什么新的和不同的？

今年的活动反映了随着 Kubernetes 的采用在日益复杂的环境中不断扩展，策略即代码格局的快速演变。最近的 Kyverno 版本引入了与新兴 Kubernetes 策略标准的更强一致性，包括扩展的基于 CEL 的策略功能以及支持跨开发和运行时环境的策略驱动工作流的改进。

与此同时，Kyverno 社区显着增长，越来越多的组织采用策略驱动的方法来大规模管理安全、治理和运营护栏。随着策略即代码成为现代平台工程的基础能力，KyvernoCon 为从业者提供了一个分享生产经验、生态系统集成和经验教训的论坛。

这一天会是什么样子e？

KyvernoCon 是一个为期半天的社区驱动活动，以技术讲座、真实案例研究、演示和互动讨论为特色。会议重点介绍组织如何应用策略即代码来管理 Kubernetes 环境中的治理、安全性和操作护栏。

该活动还为维护者、贡献者和从业者之间的对话和协作创造了空间，让与会者有机会交流想法、提出问题，并与其他在云原生生态系统中应对类似挑战的人建立联系。

与会者在离开时应该更清楚地了解如何将策略即代码应用于整个 Kubernetes 生命周期（从开发管道到生产集群），以实现治理自动化并提高安全性、可靠性和操作一致性。

通过现实案例研究和技术会议，参与者将了解组织如何实施策略驱动的方法来应对合规性、软件供应链安全和多集群平台管理等挑战。我们的活动还提供了关于政策如何成为云原生平台中不可或缺的层的观点，因为团队希望在人工智能时代安全有效地扩展 Kubernetes。

我应该先做作业吗？

无需进行大量准备，但对资源、准入控制或配置管理等 Kubernetes 概念有基本的熟悉可以帮助与会者从讨论中获得更多收获。

对于那些想要事先了解更多背景信息的人来说，查看 Kyverno 文档或尝试简单的策略可以提供有用的背景知识，并使技术会议更容易遵循，尽管我们很自豪能够提供涵盖所有知识水平的均衡演讲时间表。此外，我们将在本周晚些时候举办 ContribFest 会议，欢迎从初学者到高级用户提出更多问题和支持。请考虑在 KyvernoCon 之后加入我们继续讨论！

找到您的社区！

KyvernoCon 为用户、贡献者和维护者创建了一个专用空间，用于联系、分享经验并就政策即代码的不断发展的实践进行协作。随着 Kyverno 在组织和行业中的采用不断增长，将社区聚集在一起有助于揭示现实世界的经验教训，加强用户和维护者之间的反馈循环，并加速整个生态系统的共享学习。

此类活动还使新贡献者和从业者能够更轻松地参与该项目、发现参与机会以及与应对类似挑战的其他人建立联系。通过促进公开讨论、知识共享和协作，KyvernoCon 支持我们的全球 Kyverno 社区的持续增长和长期可持续性。有助于云原生生态系统的成熟。

不要忘记注册参加 KubeCon + CloudNativeCon Europe 2026。

item.title: 【When Kubernetes restarts your pod — And when it doesn’t】当 Kubernetes 重新启动你的 pod 时——以及当它不重新启动时
item.description:
【

A production internals guide verified against Kubernetes 1.35 GA
Companion repository: github.com/opscart/k8s-pod-restart-mechanics

The terminology problem

Engineers say “the pod restarted” when they mean four different things. Getting this wrong leads to flawed runbooks and bad on-call decisions.

The practical test: Did the pod UID change? If yes — that is recreation, not a container restart. Restart count resets to zero. If no — same pod object, container process restarted inside it.

The core insight: What kubelet actually watches

kubelet watches the pod spec — not ConfigMaps, not Secrets, not Istio CRDs. If the pod spec didn’t change, kubelet never fires. This single fact explains the majority of “why didn’t my config update?” investigations in production.

Mutating admission webhooks can change the pod spec at creation time, but never after admission — they cannot trigger container restarts post-creation.

Decision matrix


The flowchart below translates the same matrix into a decision path you can walk at 2am during an incident.

Diagram 1: Complete decision flowchart — does this change require a pod restart?

Scenario 1: ConfigMap — Why the same change has two behaviors

[Diagram 1: ConfigMap env var vs volume mount — env var pod frozen, volume pod auto-synced via kubelet symlink swap]

Env var mode (envFrom / valueFrom): The kernel copies env vars into /proc/<pid>/environ at execve(). That memory is owned by the process — no external system can modify it. Update the ConfigMap and kubelet sees no pod spec change, does nothing. The process keeps old values indefinitely.

Volume mount mode: kubelet syncs via an atomic symlink swap, not a file write:

/etc/config/
├── ..2025_12_19_11_30_00/   ← NEW data dir (kubelet creates this)
│   └── APP_COLOR            ← "red"
├── ..data ─────────────────▶ ..2025_12_19_11_30_00/  ← symlink SWAPPED atomically
└── APP_COLOR ──────────────▶ ..data/APP_COLOR

The symlink swap generates IN_CREATE on ..data — NOT IN_MODIFY on the file. Applications watching IN_MODIFY on an open file descriptor miss this entirely. This is why nginx does not auto-reload on ConfigMap changes without explicit inotify handling.

Lab Evidence (01-configmap/ in companion repo)

ConfigMap updated: APP_COLOR blue → red


Pod A (env var):      APP_COLOR=blue  ← frozen, restart count: 0
Pod B (volume mount): APP_COLOR=red   ← auto-synced, restart count: 0

Correct inotify pattern — watch the directory, not the file

watcher.Add(filepath.Dir(configPath))  // watches /etc/config/ — catches IN_CREATE
// watcher.Add(configPath)             // misses symlink swap entirely


for event := range watcher.Events {
    if event.Op&fsnotify.Create == fsnotify.Create {
        reloadConfig()
    }
}


Scenario 2: Image updates — Recreation vs container restart vs CrashLoop

These three scenarios look similar but are fundamentally different:

Successful image update — pod is recreated

BEFORE: Pod UID aaa-bbb, IP 10.244.1.5, nginx:1.25, restarts: 0
AFTER:  Pod UID xxx-yyy, IP 10.244.1.6, nginx:1.27, restarts: 0
                ↑ UID changed — RECREATION, not container restart

Diagram 3: Rolling update flow showing new ReplicaSet creation, pod recreation, and old RS retained for rollback.

ImagePullBackOff — old pod stays protected

Old pod: Running           ← Kubernetes keeps it alive
New pod: ImagePullBackOff  ← stuck, old pod never killed until new one is healthy
